plainEnglish: Don't lock your content to only portrait or only landscape. Users with a device mounted in a fixed position (for example, on a wheelchair) may not be able to rotate it, so your layout must work in both orientations—unless a specific orientation is genuinely essential, like a piano app.
whyItMatters: Users who mount a phone or tablet in a fixed orientation—common for people with motor disabilities—can't use an app that forces a single orientation.
quickCheck: Rotate your device, or toggle orientation in Chrome DevTools' device toolbar, and confirm the content still works in both portrait and landscape; axe's css-orientation-lock rule flags locking.
commonMistakes: Using a CSS transform or an @media (orientation) rule to lock or hide content in one orientation.
Setting a native orientation lock that carries into a web view.
Showing a 'please rotate your device' wall instead of supporting both orientations.

codeExamples.label: Layout adapts to any orientation
Content locked to landscape
